steps.m: Ha Meem (special letters at the start of this chapter)
steps.n: These are special letters that begin some chapters of the Quran. We pronounce each letter separately: 'Ha' then 'Meem'. While their exact meaning is known only to Allah, they show that the Quran is special and miraculous even though it uses regular Arabic letters.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb form
Changes the verb 'write' into the noun 'book' by adding an alif between second and third root letters. This word is described by المبين (the clear/manifest) in this ayah.
Makes the word definite by adding 'al' at the start
Adds 'and' to the start of the word
The word gets a kasra (i) ending and has a special alif shape (ٰ) in Quran writing style. It means 'and the book' and is part of an oath (like saying 'I swear by the book')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who/that which makes things clear (describing الكتاب)
steps.n: Changes the verb into an active participle describing something that makes things clear. Here it describes 'the Book' (الكتاب)

steps.m: the one who/that which makes things clear (describing الكتاب)
steps.n: Makes the word definite by adding 'al' at the start
Adds kasra at the end because it's an adjective describing الكتاب (the Book). The kasra shows it follows and describes الكتاب.

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes أَنزَلْنٰهُ - we sent it down)
steps.n: This is a particle that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly' in English. Here it emphasizes that 'we sent it down'.

steps.m: indeed we (subject of أَنزَلْنٰهُ - we sent it down)
steps.n: Added 'we' to show who is being emphasized. The آ at the end is a longer way of writing نا which means 'we'.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes the verb causative - showing someone is making the action happen
Adding نَا shows 'we' did the action

steps.m: we sent it down (object of إِنَّا)
steps.n: Adding هُ shows what was affected by the action (it). The word relates to إِنَّا (indeed we) in the ayah.
Basic preposition showing location or being inside something
The ى extends the sound and helps connect to the next word. This preposition tells us that something happened inside or during the night mentioned after it (لَيْلَةٍ).
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it a basic noun
Adding ة at the end makes it mean 'one night' instead of just 'night' in general
The word gets two dots under it (tanween) to show it's 'a' night, not 'the' night, and connects to the word 'blessed' after it

steps.m: to bless, to increase in goodness

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Adding alif makes the blessing more abundant/intensive
Adding مُ at the start changes it from 'blessing' to 'being blessed' - like changing from the giver to the receiver
Added ة to make it feminine since it describes لَيْلَةٍ (night) which is feminine in Arabic
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) to show indefiniteness and match the case of لَيْلَةٍ it describes. The shadda on م and long alif are pronunciation variations.
Adding shadda makes this word stronger - changing it from a simple 'if' to a strong 'indeed'

steps.m: indeed we (subject of أَنزَلْنٰهُ - we sent it down)
steps.n: Added نا at the end in the form of ا to show 'we' are doing the action. This connects to the next word أَنزَلْنٰهُ (we sent it down)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The middle letter و drops out, and we double the ن (shown by shadda) to make the word stronger. The first vowel changes from a (فتحة) to u (ضمة) sound.
Added نا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action. The word إِنَّا before it makes it act in a special way.

steps.m: to warn or give notice of danger

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show someone who does the warning
Added ين to show multiple warners. This word describes what 'we were' (كُنَّا) in the verse.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment

steps.m: in it (referring to لَيْلَة 'night' which is feminine)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to the night mentioned earlier in the Surah. This shows what is being referred to 'in it'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The يُ prefix shows something is being done to the subject rather than the subject doing the action
This root gives us words about completeness and totality
Adding the dhamma (ُ) sound to the first letter changes it into a noun meaning 'all'
Adding the final dhamma (ُ) shows this word is acting as the subject
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to transform the root into a noun meaning the result or object of commanding/directing

steps.m: a matter (object of يُفْرَقُ - is decided/distinguished)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this is an indefinite noun that is being acted upon by the verb يُفْرَقُ (is decided/distinguished) in this ayah
This root is about wisdom and good judgment
Added ي between second and third root letters to show someone/something has the quality of wisdom
Added the tanween kasra to show this word describes the matter/affair (أَمْرٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Changed from basic root letters to a noun meaning 'command' by adding marks (fatha and sukun)
Added tanween alif to show this is indefinite and is the object being described as coming 'from Us' in the ayah
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. Here it connects 'a matter' (أَمْرًا) with 'from Our presence' (عِندِنَآ)
The shadda (ّ) shows the م is pronounced more strongly because it comes after another word ending in a vowel
Added vowels to make it into a word meaning location or presence
Changed ending vowel to kasra because it comes after the preposition مِنْ
Added نَا meaning 'us/our' and lengthened the final alif for emphasis
This is a particle used to emphasize what comes after it
Added ا at the end which means 'we'. This word connects to كُنَّا (we were) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out, and vowels adjust accordingly
Doubling the middle letter makes the command stronger and more emphatic
Adding ا at the end changes it from a command to past tense 'we'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show who does the action
Added ين to make it plural (many senders). This word is receiving the action of كُنَّا (we were) in this ayah.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and form a basic noun
Added ة to show this is one specific instance of mercy
Added tanween (ً) to show this mercy is the object being given in the sentence
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows where something comes from or its source
The shadda is added because it comes after a word ending with tanween (رَحْمَةً). This is a pronunciation helper that shows the م is pronounced strongly.

steps.m: to nurture, care for, and have authority over
lord (one who nurtures and has authority)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun showing who does the action completely and perfectly
The kasra (ِ) shows this word is owned by or connected to something
The كَ at the end means 'your' (when speaking to one male). This comes after the word مِّن (from)
The shadda on the first letter (رّ) is because it comes after the word مِّن which ends with a doubled م
This particle is used to emphasize what comes after it
Added هُۥ ('he/it') at the end to show 'indeed it', referring back to Allah's mercy (رَحْمَةً) mentioned earlier

steps.m: he (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a pronoun used to refer to Allah in this context, connecting to the previous word إِنَّهُۥ (indeed He) and followed by divine attributes ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing)

steps.m: one who hears perfectly (attribute of اللَّهِ)
steps.n: This form shows someone who has this quality perfectly. When used for Allah, it means He hears everything completely.
Adding ال makes it definite and specific - here referring to Allah as The All-Hearing

steps.m: The All-Hearing (attribute of اللَّهِ, paired with ٱلْعَلِيمُ)
steps.n: Adding the ُ shows this is the subject. This divine name often appears paired with ٱلْعَلِيمُ (The All-Knowing) in the Quran.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who knows extensively (attribute of اللَّهِ)
steps.n: Changed to show this is a permanent, intensive quality of Allah
Added ٱل to make it definite and ُ to show it's describing Allah. This is one of Allah's names showing His perfect and complete knowledge of everything.

steps.m: to care for and help grow/develop
master who takes care of others (subject of السَّمٰوٰتِ)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shown by ّ) makes this mean someone who really does this action a lot - like saying 'caretaker' instead of just 'care'
Adding ِ at the end shows this word connects to the next word (السَّمٰوٰتِ/the heavens), like saying 'Lord of the heavens'
Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high/elevated
Changed سَمَاء to سَمَاوَات by adding و and ات to make it plural (more than one heaven)

steps.m: the heavens (possessed by رَبِّ - Lord of)
steps.n: Added ٱل to make it specific, doubled the س to show it connects to ٱل, and added ِ to show it belongs to رَبِّ (Lord)
Added vowels to the root letters to make the word for 'earth'
Added the definite article ال to specify a particular earth/land
Added و at beginning to connect this word to السماوات (the heavens) mentioned before it

steps.m: and the earth (possessed by/connected to رَبِّ - Lord of)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is possessed by/connected to رَبِّ (Lord of) mentioned before it
Added fatha to show the correct pronunciation
Added و at the beginning to connect this word to the previous word رَبِّ (Lord of). This shows we're adding more information about the Lord.

steps.m: to be separate from one another

steps.n: The root meaning of separation becomes a word meaning 'between' - the space that separates things. Here بَيْن is possessed by هُمَا (them two), referring to the heavens and earth, so it takes the genitive case.
Adding fatha at the end makes this word accusative, which is needed when it's used as an adverb meaning 'between'

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ and ٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Added هُمَا which means 'them both', referring to the heavens and earth mentioned before this word

steps.m: between them both (referring to السَّمٰوٰتِ and ٱلْأَرْضِ)
steps.n: Changed the final alif to a long alif (آ) for pronunciation emphasis at the end of the word

steps.m: if (introduces condition for كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ - if you are certain)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects to the phrase 'you are certain', meaning 'if you are certain'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this in the past
Changed تُ (meaning I) to تُم (meaning you all) to show many people are being addressed instead of one person

steps.m: to be completely sure about something

steps.n: Adding vowel marks to make it a past tense verb showing someone becoming certain about something
Changed it to show who does the action by adding مُ at start and و after first letter
Added emphasis by doubling the م sound

steps.m: those who are completely sure (acted on by إِن)
steps.n: Added ِينَ to show it's plural (more than two) and affected by the word إِن before it
This is a negative particle that doesn't come from a root. It negates the word إِلٰهَ (deity) that comes after it, creating the meaning 'there is no deity'.
Added fatha mark on the ل to show how it's pronounced.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This doesn't change the meaning.
Changed the first letter to إِ and added vowels to make it a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added a small alif (ٰ) after the second letter to make the 'aa' sound longer
The word gets a fatha mark at the end because لَا is saying 'there is no god' - it's a special rule when لَا means 'there is not any'
This is the base form of the exception particle that means 'except' or 'but'

steps.m: except/but (excluding anything other than هُوَ as god)
steps.n: Adding the shadda and alif makes this a complete exception particle. In this ayah, it's used to exclude anything other than Allah (هُوَ) from being god, emphasizing there is no god except Him.

steps.m: He (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun that doesn't come from a root. It's one of the basic pronouns in Arabic. In this ayah, it refers specifically to Allah as the one who gives life and causes death.
Added sukun on first letter and kasra on second letter to prepare for the causative form

steps.m: he gives life to (paired with وَيُمِيتُ - and he causes death)
steps.n: The prefix يُ shows this is happening now or in the future, and means 'he' is doing it. The ۦ at the end is a pronunciation mark for the final ي. This verb appears with وَيُمِيتُ showing Allah's power over life and death.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed from the noun 'death' to the verb 'causes death' by adding يُ at start and changing the middle letters to make it mean 'he makes something die'

steps.m: and he causes death (connected to يُحْىِۦ - gives life)
steps.n: The وَ connects this word to يُحْىِۦ (gives life) in the ayah, showing Allah's power over both life and death

steps.m: to care for, nurture, and help grow
Lord (the one who nurtures and takes care of everything)
steps.n: The doubled ب shows this is someone who really takes care of others completely. Like a parent caring for a child, but even more powerful.

steps.m: Lord (as the subject of يُحْىِۦ وَيُمِيتُ - He gives life and causes death)
steps.n: The ُ at the end shows this word is the subject (doer) of the verbs in this ayah
The كُمْ at the end shows this is talking to multiple people, like saying 'your' when speaking to a group

steps.m: to care for, raise, and look after completely
Lord (the one who takes complete care of everything)
steps.n: The doubled ب (shown by ّ) makes it mean someone who really, really takes care of everything perfectly

steps.m: and Lord (connected to رَبُّكُمْ - your Lord - from previous ayah)
steps.n: The وَ at the start means 'and', connecting this to رَبُّكُمْ (your Lord) mentioned before. The ُ at the end shows this is the main subject being talked about.

steps.m: to nurture, care for (like a father)

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic singular noun
Changed to broken plural form to show multiple fathers
Changed alif to alif madd for proper pronunciation
Added kasra to show this word is possessed by something (in this case, by 'you all')
Added كُمُ to show the fathers belong to 'you all'
Root letters أ-و-ل became أَوَّل by adding vowel marks and doubling the middle letter و to make it mean 'first'
Added ين to make it refer to a group of people who came long ago
Added ٱلْ ('the') at start and َ at end to show this refers to specific ancient people being discussed

steps.m: But rather (contradicting what came before and introducing هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ يَلْعَبُونَ)
steps.n: This is a word that means 'but rather' or 'actually'. It's used to correct what was said before and introduce a new fact. Here it introduces 'they are in doubt, playing around' to correct a previous statement.
This is a standalone pronoun that refers to a group of people being talked about. In this ayah, it's connected to the verb يَلْعَبُونَ (they are playing/amusing themselves).
Basic preposition showing location or state. Here it connects 'doubt' to the main sentence, showing they are 'in doubt'.
Added the kasra (ِ) mark under ف to show how it's pronounced (fi), and wrote ي as ى since it's at the end of the word
The middle letter is doubled to show strong/intense doubt
Added two dots under (tanween) to show it's 'a doubt' after the word 'in'
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
The يَ at the start and the ُ at the end show this is happening now or in the future
The ون ending shows multiple people (3 or more) are doing the action. This word connects to هُمْ (they) earlier in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding ا and ت at the start shows doing the watching/waiting action with more intensity and focus
Changed vowels and added sukun to make it a command

steps.m: so wait watchfully! (connected to يَوْمَ تَأْتِى)
steps.n: Added فَ at start to connect this command with what follows - the day that's mentioned next
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun form
Connected the root letters together
Added ت at beginning to make it 'she/it comes' (referring to السَّمَآءُ - the sky), and changed middle letter to match present tense pattern
Added vowel marks: fatha on ت, sukun on أ, kasra on ت, and changed ي to ى at end

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Connected the root letters س-م-و with vowels to make 'he/it rose up' in the past
Changed the verb 'to rise' into a noun 'sky' by adding ا and ء at the end - sky is what's high above us
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س because it comes after ال. This word is what's doing the action in تَأْتِى (it comes)
Added ُ at the end to show this is doing the action in the sentence
Root letters got vowels and an alif (ا) to make it mean 'smoke' instead of 'to smoke'
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with' and tanwin at end to show indefiniteness
Added مُ at start to show it's happening now, and changed vowels to make it mean 'something that makes things clear'
Added shadda to emphasize the clarity/evidence
Added tanween to show this describes the smoke (دُخَانٍ) mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning to show this is happening in the present. The sukun on غ helps with pronunciation.
Added ى at the end because this is a special type of verb that ends with ى
Forms a noun meaning 'social beings' which became the common word for people
Adds emphasis to show this refers to all people as a group
Adds definite article 'the'. This word is being acted upon by يَغْشَى (it covers/overwhelms)
The fatha ending shows this word is receiving the action of being covered/overwhelmed
This is the basic pointing word that shows something is near
Added ذا to make it specifically point to a masculine thing that's near
Added the alif mark (ٰ) and fatha marks to show proper pronunciation. This word is pointing to the punishment (عَذَابٌ) mentioned right after it in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
This pattern changes the verb into a noun meaning the result of the action. Here it describes what 'this' (هٰذَا) is - it is a punishment.
Adding the double dammah makes it 'a punishment' instead of just 'punishment'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
This form creates an intensive adjective describing something that causes a lot of pain. Here it describes the punishment (عَذَابٌ) mentioned in the ayah.
Adding tanween dammah makes the noun indefinite

steps.m: to care for and help grow
he cared for and helped grow
steps.n: Adding fatha marks turns it into a past tense verb meaning 'he did this action'

steps.m: O our Lord! (calling out to اكْشِفْ)
steps.n: Root 'rababa' became 'rabbana' - adding 'na' means 'our Lord' and we say 'O' before it to call out to Allah
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed to imperative command form by adding alif and changing middle vowel
Adding sukun makes it a proper command form
This is a basic word that shows movement or separation away from something
Added نا at the end to show this is happening to 'us'. This word is connected to اكْشِفْ (remove) in the ayah, showing what to remove something away from.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from the action 'punished' to the noun 'punishment' - like changing 'hit' to 'a hit'
The ٱلْ makes it specific (like 'the') and the َ shows it's being acted upon by the verb 'remove'
This is a special word that adds emphasis to statements. It doesn't come from a root.
The ا at the end is actually part of نا which means 'we'. This shows who the emphasis is about in this ayah where people are asking Allah to remove punishment from them.

steps.m: to trust, feel safe, be faithful

steps.n: Added مُ at the start and vowels to show this describes someone who does the action of believing
Added ُونَ at the end to show we're talking about multiple believers. This word connects to إِنَّا (indeed we) in the ayah to show who 'we' are.
This is the basic interrogative particle
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning

steps.m: How can it be? From where? (expressing disbelief about الذِّكْرَىٰ)
steps.n: Adding ى with alif maksura (ىٰ) changes this into a question word expressing surprise or disbelief. In this ayah, it questions how they could benefit from the reminder (الذِّكْرَىٰ) when they had already rejected a clear messenger.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound
The word 'for' (لَ) got 'them' (هم) attached to it, making it mean 'for them'. The ُ (u-sound) shows it connects to the next word 'the reminder'
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to transform verb into noun meaning 'remembrance'
Added ىٰ at the end to make the word stronger, like saying 'a big reminder' instead of just 'reminder'

steps.m: the remembrance (that they were warned about)
steps.n: Added the definite article ٱل and doubled the first letter with shadda
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation - fatha on ق and sukun on د

steps.m: and certainly (connects to جَآءَهُمْ - came to them)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what follows. In this ayah, it connects 'certainly' to the phrase 'came to them a clear messenger'

steps.m: to arrive, to come to someone/something

steps.n: Basic past tense verb pattern with fatha marks

steps.m: he came to them (object of came)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who he came to. This word connects to رَسُولٌ (messenger) which comes after it in the ayah.
The و makes it mean someone who does this action a lot
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is referring to any messenger, not a specific one
Adds مُ at the start to show who/what does the action, and ِ in the middle to complete the pattern
The ٌ shows this word is independent and not defined by 'the'

steps.m: clearly evident, very clear (describing رَسُولٌ - a messenger)
steps.n: The ّ on م makes the meaning stronger. This word describes the messenger (رَسُولٌ) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: then (connects to تَوَلَّوْا - they turned away)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that shows sequence - it tells us that after something happened before, they turned away. The shadda (ّ) on م makes the meaning stronger.
Connected the root letters with basic vowels to make it a past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling) on ل makes the turning action more intense
Adding تَ at start shows he did the action to himself

steps.m: they turned away (from عَنْهُ - from him)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did this action. This word connects to عَنْهُ which shows who they turned away from.
This is a word that shows direction or relation
Added هُ at the end to show who is being moved away from. This connects to the verb تَوَلَّوْا (they turned away) in the ayah.
Added fatha marks and the middle و changed to ا because it's a hollow verb. This makes it past tense.
Adding وا makes it plural masculine - meaning more than one man did the action

steps.m: and they said (subject of مُعَلَّمٌ مَّجْنُونٌ)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This word is connected to مُعَلَّمٌ مَّجْنُونٌ (taught one, possessed) which comes after it in the ayah.
The root letters show the basic idea of knowledge
Adding shadda makes it mean 'to teach' with emphasis, like teaching thoroughly or repeatedly

steps.m: one who is taught (object of قَالُوا - they said)
steps.n: Adding مُ at start and ٌ at end shows this describes someone who receives the teaching. In this ayah, this word is what they said (object of قَالُوا).

steps.m: to hide or cover the mind/senses
steps.n: The root relates to being hidden or covered, which came to mean crazy because the mind is 'covered'

steps.m: one whose mind is hidden/covered (object of قَالُوا)
steps.n: The prefix مَ and pattern with و show this is happening to someone. This word is what they said (object of قَالُوا - they said).
The shadda on م makes the word stronger, emphasizing the state of being mentally covered/possessed. This is what they called him after turning away.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain
The ا at the end means 'we/us'. This word connects to كَاشِفُوا (removers) in this ayah, showing who will be doing the removing.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
This form shows who is doing the action. The word is the subject of إِنَّ (inna) which comes at the start of the ayah.

steps.m: we are the removers (subject of إِنَّا)
steps.n: Changed from singular 'remover' to plural 'removers' by adding وا at the end to show multiple people doing the action

steps.m: punishment (object of كاشفوا - we will remove)
steps.n: Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun describing the result of punishment. This word is being acted upon by كاشفوا (we will remove).

steps.m: the punishment (object of كاشفوا - we will remove)
steps.n: Adding ال makes it specific - this is talking about a particular punishment.
The ي makes it into an adjective describing something that has the quality of being small in amount

steps.m: for a little while (object of كَاشِفُوا/removing)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this describes how long the removing will last. It's connected to the word كَاشِفُوا (removing) in the ayah.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain
Added كُمْ at the end to show this is addressing multiple people. This word is the object of كَاشِفُوا۟ (removers) in this ayah, showing who the removal will affect.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
The و changes to ئ because it comes after ا. This describes who is doing the action. It's telling us about 'you all' (إِنَّكُمْ)
The ونَ makes it plural for masculine

steps.m: a day (object of نَبْطِشُ - we seize)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters. This word tells when the action نَبْطِشُ (we seize) happens.

steps.m: on the day (object of نَبْطِشُ - we seize)
steps.n: The ending َ shows this word answers 'when?' for the verb نَبْطِشُ (we seize), like saying 'on that day we will seize'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: like boiling (of الْحَمِيمِ - the scalding fluid)
steps.n: Added كَ at the beginning to mean 'like' and ِ at the end because it's connected to الْحَمِيمِ (the scalding fluid) in the next word
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this water is extremely hot. The pattern shows something has a very strong quality.

steps.m: the boiling hot water (like كَغَلْىِ - like boiling)
steps.n: Added ال at the beginning to make it specific and ِ at the end to show it connects with the word before it. This word describes the extremely hot water that's mentioned as 'like boiling' in this verse.

steps.m: to grasp or take hold of

steps.n: Changed from basic verb 'to take' into a command by dropping أ and adding a vowel mark (like saying 'Take this!')
Added و to make it plural - now addressing multiple people
Added هُ to show who should be taken. This word is connected to فَٱعْتِلُوهُ (drag him) in the next part of the verse, showing a sequence of commands about what to do with this person.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Removed first vowel and changed middle vowel to make it ready for a command
Added imperative alif to make it a command
Added فَ to connect this command with the previous one (خُذُوهُ - take him)
Added وا to show this is a command to multiple people

steps.m: and drag him roughly! (command to group)
steps.n: Added هُ (him) at the end to show who is being dragged roughly
This is a basic directional word that shows movement or connection towards something. In this ayah, it connects 'take him' (خُذُوهُ) with 'the middle' (سَوَآءِ) of Hell.
Added a small alif (ىٰ) at the end instead of regular ى. This is just a writing style - it doesn't change the pronunciation or meaning.

steps.m: to be in the middle, to be equal, to be balanced

steps.n: Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'state of being equal'

steps.m: to the middle, to the center (of الْجَحِيمِ - the Hellfire)
steps.n: The kasra ending shows this word follows إِلَىٰ (to) in the ayah, indicating movement towards the middle of Hellfire
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show intensity, and changed middle vowel to kasra
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific Hellfire mentioned in the Quran
Added kasra at the end to show this describes something's relationship to the Hellfire
This is a connecting word that shows what happens next in a sequence. In this ayah, it connects to the command صُبُّوا۟ (pour), showing what should be done after something else.
Changed first vowel to damma to make it a command
Added وا to make it a command to a group (in this ayah, angels are commanded to pour boiling water)
Changes from verb meaning to direction/location word
Added fatha at the end to show this word is describing a location. This word connects to رَأْسِهِ (his head) to tell us where something is happening.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it a usable word
Added i-sound (kasra) at the end because it's connected to the word 'over' before it
Added هِۦ at the end to show the head belongs to him. The ۦ is just a pronunciation mark for the هِ

steps.m: from (connects عَذَابِ to what comes before it)
steps.n: This is a preposition that connects two parts of the sentence, showing that something comes from عَذَابِ (punishment). In this ayah, it's used to specify which type of punishment is being poured.
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun meaning 'punishment'. This word is connected to الْحَمِيمِ (the boiling water) to show what kind of punishment.
The ِ at the end shows this word is connected to الْحَمِيمِ (the boiling water), like saying 'punishment of the boiling water'
Added vowels and ي to make it mean something that has this quality strongly

steps.m: the intensely hot water (object of مِنْ عَذَابِ - from the punishment of)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ِ to show it's connected to the words 'from the punishment of' that come before it
Connected the letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form

steps.m: taste! (command to one person, said mockingly to the arrogant person in Hell)
steps.n: Changed to command form by: 1) Adding damma on first letter, 2) Dropping the middle weak letter و, 3) Adding sukun on last letter. This is said as a harsh command to the person who used to think they were mighty and noble.
This is an emphasis particle that starts sentences to add certainty

steps.m: indeed you (being mocked by ذُقْ - taste!)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end which means 'you' (when speaking to one male). This is being said mockingly to someone, telling them to 'taste' (ذُقْ) punishment, saying 'indeed you' are the mighty and noble one (sarcastically).

steps.m: you (speaking to one male, subject of ٱلْعَزِيزُ)
steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun used for emphasis. In this ayah, it's being used sarcastically to mock someone who thought they were mighty, telling them to 'taste [punishment] - indeed, you are the mighty, the noble.'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Adding ي in the middle makes this an intensive adjective - showing someone/something has a lot of this quality
Adding ٱلْ makes it definite (like 'the' in English). The ُ shows this is describing 'you' in the phrase 'you are the Mighty One'

steps.m: to be generous, noble, or honorable

steps.n: Added vowels to make it mean 'he was' noble/generous in the past
Changed pattern to show this is a permanent quality

steps.m: The Noble, Generous One (used sarcastically in this ayah to mock the person who thought they were noble and mighty)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it definite and ُ to show it's describing the subject. In this ayah, it's used sarcastically with العزيز to mock someone who thought they were mighty and noble.
This is the basic form of the word that can mean 'if' or 'whether'
Adding the shadda changes this from a conditional word to an emphasis word that makes what comes after it more certain
This is the basic attention-getting part that starts many pointing words
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's shortened when speaking
The ذ with fatha makes it specifically point to something close by

steps.m: this (pointing to something nearby) (subject of مَا)
steps.n: The final alif makes it able to point to masculine things. In this ayah, it's the subject of مَا which follows it.

steps.m: that which (refers to what is being doubted in كُنتُم بِهِۦ تَمْتَرُونَ)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it refers back to what was mentioned before and connects it to what people were doubting.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Command form ('be!') made by dropping the middle letter و and adding a sukun at the end
Added ت to make the verb past tense and indicate the subject (you/I)

steps.m: you all were (referring to what you used to doubt)
steps.n: Changed from 'I was' to 'you all were' by adding م at the end to make it plural, showing what the group used to doubt
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or what tool is used
Added the pronoun هِ to show who/what the preposition refers to
Added the small ۦ mark after هِ to help with pronunciation. This word is connected to تَمْتَرُونَ (you all doubt), showing what is being doubted.
Changed the final ى to ي to prepare for additional endings
Added تَ at the beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Added ت after م to make the word stronger and more emphatic, showing strong doubt
Added ون to show this is about a group of people doing the action right now

This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the God-conscious people (ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ) are definitely in a secure place.

steps.m: to guard against harm or evil
steps.n: Basic past tense form with weak letter ي at the end
The root letters were connected by adding vowel marks (َ). The last letter ي became ى because it has a fatha sound at the end.

steps.m: one who guards themselves against evil
steps.n: Changed from a basic verb (to guard) into a noun meaning 'someone who guards themselves'. The و and ت merged to make تّ with a double mark.

steps.m: those who guard themselves against evil
steps.n: Added ين to make it plural (more than two people)

steps.m: the God-fearing ones (who are in مَقَامٍ أَمِينٍ - a secure position)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific. In this ayah, it refers to people who will be in a secure position.
This is a basic preposition showing where the righteous (المتقين) will be. It connects to مَقَامٍ (position/place) to show they will be in a secure position.
The ي changes to ى because it's at the end of how we say the word by itself. This doesn't change the meaning.
The root letters show the basic idea of standing or rising up

steps.m: place of standing (location in أَمِينٍ secure)
steps.n: Added مَ at the start to show where something happens, and added ا after the middle letter to make it a place noun

steps.m: a place of standing (location in أَمِينٍ secure)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is any place of standing, not a specific one
This is the basic past tense form of the verb

Added ي between first and second root letters with vowel marks to create an adjective meaning 'having the quality of being trustworthy'. This word describes the station/place (مَقَامٍ) mentioned in the ayah.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) at the end to match the grammar of مَقَامٍ that it describes
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something
Added kasra (ِ) under ف to show it's connecting to the word after it
Changed the ي to ى which is just a writing style change - it means the same thing
Connected the root letters and added fatha marks to show past tense action

steps.m: a garden (a place covered with vegetation)
steps.n: Adding ة (taa marbouta) to the end turns the verb 'to cover' into a noun meaning 'a covered place/garden'
Adding ات makes it plural. This word comes after فِى (in/within) in the ayah.
The word gets a special ending (ٍ) because it comes after فِى (in/at/on), showing where something is located
The root relates to seeing (eye) and flowing (spring)
Added the vowel mark 'a' sound (fatha) to make it mean a single eye or water spring
Changed the 'a' sound to 'u' sound to start making it plural
Added 'u' sound after second letter to continue plural pattern
Added و after second letter to make full plural form
Added two dots under the last letter to show this word is receiving an action (like saying 'from springs' instead of just 'springs')
Added و with fatha to connect this word to 'gardens' mentioned before it
Basic past tense verb meaning 'to wear' or 'to put on'
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they put on (clothing) (subject of مُّتَقٰبِلِينَ - facing each other)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people doing the action. This describes the people who are facing each other (مُّتَقٰبِلِينَ) in paradise.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or what it's made of

steps.m: of/from (describing what يَلْبَسُونَ 'they wear' is made from)
steps.n: Added kasra (ِ) under م to show this word connects to the next word. Here it shows what the garments are made from.
This is a borrowed word for a specific type of silk. The tanwin (double dots under) shows it's indefinite
Added damma marks to help with pronunciation
Added tanwin kasra (double dots under) at the end to show this is indefinite and being used as part of a description (they wear garments of fine silk)
This is a borrowed word from Persian meaning luxurious silk brocade. Adding the vowel marks helps with correct pronunciation.
Adding وَ at the start connects this word to the previous items mentioned (fine silk/سُندُس) that the people of Paradise will wear
Adding tanwin kasra (ٍ) shows this is part of what comes after مِن (from/of) in the verse
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
Added تَ at beginning and ا after second letter to show mutual action between people
Changed from a past tense verb (they faced) to a descriptive word (facing) to show current state
Added emphasis on first letter with shadda

steps.m: those facing each other (describes the people wearing garments)
steps.n: Added ينَ to show this describes multiple people who are wearing (يَلْبَسُونَ) the garments
Starting with the demonstrative pronoun ذا meaning 'this/that'
Added كَ at the beginning to show similarity or comparison

steps.m: like that, in that way (describing how وَزَوَّجْنٰهُم 'We will pair them' happens)
steps.n: Added لِكَ to make it mean 'like that' - points to how something will happen (similar to saying 'this way' while pointing)
The root becomes a noun meaning one who is paired/joined with another
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Adding shadda makes it mean actively causing the pairing/marriage rather than just joining
Added نٰ to show 'we' did the action, with sukun before it
Added وَ to connect this action with what came before it

steps.m: and we married them (referring to the believers mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هُم to show who was married
The root letters are shaped into a plural noun pattern that describes a group with this characteristic
The root letters form a plural noun حُور meaning 'companions with beautiful eyes' - like changing 'beautiful eye' into 'people with beautiful eyes'
The بِ at the beginning adds the meaning of 'with'

steps.m: with companions of beautiful eyes (in a general sense)
steps.n: The tanween at the end (ٍ) shows this is indefinite, meaning we're talking about companions in general

steps.m: eye, or having beautiful wide eyes
steps.n: The word means both a physical eye and the essence or reality of something
Adding the kasra vowel makes this a plural noun pattern
The tanwin shows this is describing the حُورٍ (maidens) mentioned earlier in the verse
Added vowels to connect letters. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
The يَ at the start shows it's happening now/future. The ُو ending is because it's a weak verb.
Adding نَ makes it plural - shows multiple people are doing the action
Added the kasra (ِ) vowel mark to show how to pronounce the word

Added ها at the end to show what is being referred to. Here it refers to Paradise (which is feminine in Arabic). The full context is that the believers will be calling for all kinds of fruit while in Paradise.
Added damma and shadda to make the noun meaning 'all' or 'every'
Added kasra to show this word is connected to what comes after it (فٰكِهَةٍ - fruit)
Added bi- prefix to show 'with'. This word connects to فٰكِهَةٍ (fruit) in the ayah, meaning 'with every fruit'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense meaning
Added ا after first letter and ة at end to create a noun for something characterized by enjoyment

steps.m: any fruit (in a series of blessings)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra to show this is one item in a list of blessings being described. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this Quranic spelling.
Added vowel marks to show 'he' did the action in the past
Changed from a past tense verb (he felt safe) to describe someone who has safety/security

steps.m: ones who are safe (describing the believers in Paradise)
steps.n: Added ين ending to show this describes multiple people in Paradise who are feeling safe and secure
This is a negative particle that makes the action that follows it not happen. In this ayah, it's saying they will not taste death.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding يَ at start shows present tense, and vowel pattern changes because of the middle weak letter و

steps.m: they taste/experience (referring to the people of Paradise)
steps.n: Adding ونَ makes it refer to a group of people. This word is negated by لَا before it, so it means 'they will not taste'
Added kasra vowel mark to show proper pronunciation

steps.m: in it (referring to Paradise mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added ها to show this refers to Paradise (الجنة) which was mentioned earlier. The ها is feminine because الجنة is a feminine word.
Changed from action (to die) to the thing itself (death) by adding vowel marks and connecting letters

steps.m: the death (object of يَذُوقُونَ - they taste)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to make it specific and َ at the end because it's the object being tasted in the phrase 'they do not taste death'

This is a basic exception particle that shows something is different from what came before. In this ayah, it shows that the first death is an exception to the general rule that they won't taste death in Paradise.

steps.m: except/but (connects to لَا يَذُوقُونَ - they will not taste)
steps.n: The alif at the end helps connect this word smoothly to what comes after it in the sentence
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun for death
Transforms from general concept of death to a specific instance
Adding definite article makes it 'the specific death'. This word is the object of يَذُوقُونَ (they taste) in this ayah.

steps.m: to come first or be at the beginning

steps.n: Changed the root letters into a common word meaning 'first' by adding the vowels u and a
Added ى at the end to make it feminine
Added ال to make it specific and the small alif (ٰ) for proper pronunciation

steps.m: to protect or shield from harm

steps.n: Root letters joined together with fathas to make 'he protected' - this is the one who protects others from tasting something in this verse
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added alif maksura (ى with line on top) because word ends in ي
Added هُمْ at end to show who was protected
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past

steps.m: punishment (object of وَقَىٰهُمْ - protected them from)
steps.n: Adding ا after the second letter changes this from the action of punishing to the punishment itself. This word is what Allah protected the believers from, connected to وَقَىٰهُمْ (protected them).
Added ي between second and third root letters and vowel marks to create a noun meaning a place or thing with this intense quality
Adding definite article and genitive case ending

steps.m: to give extra, to bestow favor

steps.n: The root letters are connected with vowel marks to create a noun meaning the result of giving favor

steps.m: as a favor (from رَّبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: The ًا at the end shows this describes how something is given - as a favor. This connects to the next words 'from your Lord'.
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows where something comes from or belongs to
The shadda (ّ) is added because this word comes after a sukun, making the pronunciation clearer and connecting it smoothly to the next word

steps.m: to care for and help grow

steps.n: The root 'to care for' becomes 'my Lord' - the ِ ending shows someone is calling out to their Lord
Adding كَ at the end makes it mean 'your Lord' (when speaking to one male)
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something close by
The alif is written with a special mark (alif khanjariya) to show it's stretched in pronunciation
Adding ل with kasra (ِ) changes it from pointing to something near to pointing to something far

steps.m: that, that one (pointing to something far)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end makes it more specific, like saying 'that one' instead of just 'that'
This is a demonstrative pronoun that points to what was just mentioned. In this ayah, it refers back to the favor (فَضْلًا) from your Lord that was just described.
Changed from action to noun meaning the result of winning. This word describes what 'that' (هُوَ) is in the phrase 'that is the great success'.
Added damma to indicate nominative case
Added definite article to make the noun definite
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from a past tense verb (he became mighty) to a describing word (mighty/magnificent)

steps.m: The Magnificent One (describing الْفَوْزُ - the triumph)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific and ُ to show it describes الْفَوْزُ (the triumph) mentioned in the ayah
This is the base emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added مَا at the end to limit or specify what comes after (like saying 'only' or 'just')
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
The shadda doubles the س to show making something easy (causative), rather than just being easy
Adding نا at the end changes 'he' to 'We' (Allah speaking). This word connects back to إنَّ at the start of the verse.

steps.m: We made it easy (referring to the Quran)
steps.n: The هُ at the end means 'it' - referring to the Quran that was made easy. This verb is part of a statement beginning with فَإِنَّمَا (then indeed/so certainly).
Related to speech and language
Forms a noun for the physical tongue or language in general
Added بِ at beginning to show how something is done

steps.m: with your tongue/language (used to make يَسَّرْنٰهُ easier)
steps.n: Added كَ (your) at the end. This whole phrase explains how Allah made something (يَسَّرْنٰهُ) easier - He did it using your language.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. لَعَلَّ is a particle that shows hope or expectation for something to happen.

steps.m: perhaps they/so that they (subject of يَتَذَكَّرُونَ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show this applies to 'them'. This word connects to يَتَذَكَّرُونَ (they remember/take heed) in the next part of the ayah, showing hope that they will remember or take heed.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added تَ at start and doubled middle letter to show they're doing deep reflection on their own

steps.m: he reflects deeply (on لَعَلَّهُمْ 'so that they')
steps.n: Changed to present tense with يَ at start and ُ at end. This word comes after لَعَلَّهُمْ (so that they) in the ayah.

steps.m: they reflect deeply (on لَعَلَّهُمْ 'so that they')
steps.n: Added ون to show many people are doing the reflecting. This matches with هُمْ (they) in لَعَلَّهُمْ (so that they) from earlier in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Added ا and ت at the start to show doing the watching/waiting for oneself with extra focus
Changed from 'he waited' to 'you wait!' by changing the last vowel and adding a stop mark (sukun) to make it a command
Added فَ at beginning to connect this command with what came before
This is a particle that adds emphasis to the word مُّرْتَقِبُونَ (watching/waiting). It's like saying 'truly' or 'definitely' in English.
Added هُم which means 'they' (for a group of males). This connects to the word مُّرْتَقِبُونَ (watching/waiting) to show who is doing the watching.
Added vowels (a-a-a) to make it 'he watched' in the past tense

steps.m: one who is waiting and watching
steps.n: Added مُ at start and ت after first root letter to show someone doing the action to themselves, with ِ on ق to show it's happening now

steps.m: ones who are waiting and watching (subject of فَٱرْتَقِبْ)
steps.n: Added shadda on م for emphasis and ون at the end to show multiple people. This word relates to فَٱرْتَقِبْ (so wait) in the ayah, showing who is doing the waiting.
